Throughout the week, the participant did not mention the stories, and there was no observable impact on anger regulation or negative emotions. The stories are fully memorized. Pauses during responses increased, as the participant appeared to think before answering. This can be considered a positive change, as in previous sessions incorrect responses were often given quickly and without reflection.Although he answered the questions, overall impact on his functional anger control remained limited..

The participant had fully memorized the story and recounted it from the beginning of the session. The focus throughout the session was on enhancing creativity, and role-play techniques were used to facilitate internalization of the story.
The stories have been effective so far, but he requires external guidance and prompts.
In one instance of very intense anger, he was able to control himself. This had not occurred previously and, in the past, such situations resulted in physical aggression toward me.

It should be stated at the outset that the parents demonstrated poor compliance with assigned responsibilities and commitments. After each request from the therapist, they repeatedly postponed the submission of required documentation and disrupted the proper course of the study through various justifications. Up to the final session, they refused to submit the informed consent form and parent-report questionnaires, and none of the requested home-based activities were carried out. As a result, no consent form or parent reports are available for this case, and no significant improvement in treatment effectiveness was observed in the participant.
To keep the meeting from getting boring, we tried to tell the story together and create variety.
He has understood all the stories completely. He greeted me when he entered the class today.
He listened to the story with the interest.

This week, Fardin experienced hormonal dysregulation, and I attempted to calm him by using the story techniques and reminding him of the story itself. During moments of anger, when I ask him to think about the story, the strategy is highly successful.

Her level of cooperation is low and she is being oppositional. I think she learns everything but deliberately chooses not to respond. The participant continued to resist story reading. It appears that the use of visual and auditory stimuli would be more appropriate for this participant in order to engage multiple sensory modalities.

The first story has been retained and stabilized in his memory, and he answers questions based on that story. According to the mother, he has applied the key message of the story two to three times in similar real-life situations.

According to the family, the stories have been effective thus far. Abtins anxiety has decreased, and he separates more easily from his parents when going to familiar places. He also demonstrates very good cooperation and engagement during class.

The stories are appropriate and include details that are engaging for Amirhossein. Learning and performance during the session are satisfactory; however, in similar situations, he still requires reminders and prompts.
He still becomes angry; however, both the duration and intensity of his anger have decreased.
Performance is satisfactory but not spontaneous.

According to the family, the intervention has had some positive effects so far; however, these effects appear to depend on Amir Hosseins emotional state. When he is not dysregulated or anxious, he is able to comply more effectively and consistently.

He rapidly retold all four stories with full detail and without any errors. While strong memory is a common characteristic in individuals with autism, the ability of this method to capture and sustain his attention is particularly noteworthy. He learned three techniques and attempted to implement them.
The only positive change observed was a reduced tendency to leave the classroom. The participant remained seated and visually attentive but demonstrated no verbal participation.
Marked restlessness was observed, and cooperation was absent.

He has understood that resolving conflicts with others requires finding appropriate, non aggressive solutions.

At the beginning of the session, he said he had forgotten all the stories, and by reminding them he remembered them all. He used the sentences " I can stay untill the end of the class".
He engaged in behaviours such as hair pulling and wrist pressing during the session, which were attributed to fatigue from school.

At the start of the session, the participant moved toward the classroom blackboard and displayed behaviors indicative of inattention. As the session progressed, the participant became engaged with the story and at the end of the session stated, It was really cool.
He understood that greeting others is important. He says greeting in a low voice.
She says I really enjoy it when a new story is read to me.
Amirhossein demonstrates good participation and cooperation throughout the session. He understands the key points of the stories and attempts to apply them.
The participant demonstrated a very high learning speed and answered all questions correctly.
The participant was able to fully retell the story independently. Sustained attention during story listening increased.
The participant expressed positive emotions when answering questions correctly. This highlights the importance of story repetition sessions for participants with lower functional levels.
Establishing connections between the events of the story remained difficult for the participant. He required extensive cues and guidance to respond to the questions.

I asked him how he felt about the storytelling sessions we had together. He responded, I am strong and I can stand up for myself, and I am calm and I defend my rights. His body language has improved, and he attempts to use an assertive communication style in his speech. The father reported improved engagement with the stories and noted that the participant has requested stories in which he himself is the main character. This request indicates increased interest resulting from hearing his own name, interests, and successes reflected in the stories.
